content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. What's the most intelligent, mind-bending movie you've ever watched?

**Score:** 200
**Body:** I'm looking for mind-bending thriller or mystery movies with intelligent plots and a dark, atmospheric vibe.

I'm a big fan of movies that:

* Keep you guessing from start to finish
* Have major twists or shocking reveals
* Feature psychological, mystery, or sci-fi elements
* Make you question what's real
* Have a moody, immersive atmosphere
* Stay with you long after the credits roll

I'm not necessarily looking for action-heavy films. I prefer movies that feel like a puzzle and gradually reveal their secrets.

What are the best mind-bending movies you've seen that fit this description?

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. # Hard to Be a God (2013 film)

A team of scientists travels from Earth to the planet Arkanar, which is inhabited by a race of humans identical to those of Earth. Their civilization has not progressed beyond a civilization that is culturally and technologically nearly identical to medieval Europe. One reason may be the militantly anti-intellectual attitude of Arkanar: Anyone whom the inhabitants of the planet consider to be an "intellectual" is instantly executed. The Earth scientists are ordered not to interfere and to conceal their identities; but one of them, Rumata, wishes to stop the senseless murders of brilliant minds and is forced at last to pick a side.
2. The prestige was the only movie I could never guess what was going to happen.

Its a slow burn of a movie and kinda long but the ending absolutely blew my mind. 

The ending will make you think back to so many moments that didn't feel like they made any sense at the time but then it clicks like holy shit. Lol.
3. Coherence
4. Primer and Predestination, both spring immediately to mind
  - Predestination. Holy shit that’s a great movie. 
5. Pi 
6. The Prestige (2006)
  - I saw *The Prestige* alone at an opening Friday matinee, went home, gathered up my then girlfriend and her kids, and took them all to the evening showing. "You have to see this before someone ruins it for you."
7. Synechdoche: New York 
8. Vanilla Sky

## 2. Movies for a lonely guy

**Score:** 19
**Body:** That’s me, a lonely loser. I’m sitting on my couch in my underwear with my bong anxious about work tomorrow give me a movie. Really can be about anything but i would prefer uplifting. Now gimme those suggestions \*hand grabbing motion\*

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The station agent is amazing
2. The Secret Life of Walter Mitty 
  - This or Yes Man
3. Limitless
4. Free Guy
5. Her 
6. A Man Named Otto 
7. Chef

Amelie
8. Lost in Translation 

The Lonely Guy
9. The Holdovers.
......

Sunday /pre-Monday dread is real.

Getting up & getting dressed will help.

Going outside of your 4 walls ~ for a walk, or to get a coffee & look at scenery~  will help.

Stop doomscrolling by 10pm and go to bed.
.......

Hang in there.

## 3. movies like Coraline

**Score:** 17
**Body:** My daughter and I love this movie. It's just perfect, gives this feeling like we're completely alone in the world. Other movies we like (random) include spirited away, inception, nerve, inglourious Basterds (strange list, I am aware). But specifically I would like recommendations like Coraline because we adore it.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Monster House

James and The Giant Peach

Kubo and the Two Strings

9

Miss Peregrine's Home for Peculiar Children 

Where the Wild Things Are 

My Neighbor Totoro

Pan's Labyrinth

Bridge to Terabithia 

  - Woah…Pan’s Labyrinth is \*not\* like the others on this list. It’s an amazing film (my favorite from the list you posted), but it’s much darker and more violent. 

Since they like Inglorious Basterds, I wouldn’t call it inappropriate, but it’s worth pointing out that it’s not really like Coraline or the other films on your list. 
    - ....
I'm going to be honest, I haven't seen it in many many years and I might have confused it with Labyrinth 😅 

Thanks for pointing it out!!!
2. Paranorman is pretty solid.
3. Secret of Kells (2009)  
Song of the Sea (2014)  
The Breadwinner (2017)  
Wolfwalkers (2020)  
Kiki's Delivery Service (1989)  
Ponyo (2008)  
Howl's Moving Castle (2004)  
The Secret World of Arrietty (2010)  
Scavengers Reign (2023 ) - Tv Series  
Arcane (2021) - Tv Series - Warning: last few episodes infer sexuality  
Flow (2024)
4. Corpse Bride

Perfect Blue
5. Maybe the movie "9" or even "Wall-E"????

Not like Coraline, but Cast Away has the Tom Hanks character alone for most of the movie; similarly Will Smith in I am Legend for much of that.
6. Moon Garden
7. Frankenweenie is a veryyyyy good movie 🎥 really similar to coraline just that this one is in black and white 10/10 u will love it ⭐️🫶🏻
8. Hunt for the Wilderpeople

The Secret Garden

The Secret of Roan Inish

## 4. Low budget cult classic superhero films.

**Score:** 14
**Body:** We are planning a movie night and I'm looking for "The Room (2003)" equivalent of superhero films. Something a group of friends can enjoy while being drunk. Films which are so-bad-its-good type but made with full heart. 

Edit: Thank you for all the suggestions guys, We are reading them all, keep them coming. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. [Fantastic Four](https://boxd.it/1B70) (1994)
2. Mystery Men

or if you want something low low budget and utterly mad then 'Rat Pfink a Boo Boo' is the nuts!
3. Check out the toxic avenger
4. Dr. Horrible's Sing Along Blog. 
5. Condorman (1981)
6. The Punisher (1989)
7. Darkman
8. Orgazmo

9. Tank Girl
10. Movies like *The Toxic Avenger* (1984) and *The Fantastic Four* (1994) will be more fun for your purposes, (Toxie also has a whole franchise) but the worst one of all time is hands down *The Amazing Bulk*. It was shot in 5 days on a $14,000 budget and has an IMDb score of 1.8 (reminder that you cannot rate "0" and go below "1") It's also only 78 minutes.

Dark horse candidate, but most people forget to include *RoboCop* (1987) but it completely qualifies as a superhero film. The original is actually good, but the sequels are the trash you're looking for if you want to marathon the trilogy

The *Batman* movie from 1966 is fun camp.

Robert Townsend's *Meteor Man* (1993) - an effort to create a positive black super hero, and rope in dozens of cameos, like James Earl Jones, Sinbad, Big Daddy Kane, Eddie Griffin, Luther Vandross, Bill Cosby (ew), Don Cheadle 30 years younger, Frank Gorshin (Batman 66's Riddler), and Wallace Shawn ("Inconcievable!")

*Blankman* (1994) an extension of Damon Wayans' mentally handi-capped *In Living Color* superhero Handi-Man. This one's perfectly middle-of-the-road and has a small cult following.

*Flash Gordon* (1980) is intentional camp with an incredible soundtrack by Queen and is a ton of fun when you just go along for the ride. The protagonist is literally just a football quarterback who takes on the interdimensional armies of Ming the Merciless. A large amount of white powder was involved in making this movie.

## 5. Dark comedies about lonely people

**Score:** 9
**Body:** I’m looking for films in the same vein as *Happiness* (1998), *Frownland* (2007), and *What Happened Was…* (1994) that explore the human condition and loneliness in comedically uncomfortable ways. Bonus points if a lot of the humor derives from realistically awkward dialogue scenes between characters and if it has an unhappy ending!

Thanks!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Lobster is perfect 
2. Anomalisa
3. Harrold and Maud. 
4. A Man Called Ove


If you're not into subtitles they remade it with Tom Hanks (A Man Called Otto) but I haven't seen that so can't vouch for the levels of cynicism.
5. The Cable Guy 
6. The Station Agent
7. The Whale
8. Lars and the Real Girl
9. Her
10. Lars and the Real Girl (2007)

$9.99 (2008)

The Double (2013)

Sometimes I Think About Dying (2023)

Friendship (2024)

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Which movie made you cringe the most?

**Score:** 974
**Body:** For me, The Drama (2026) 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Emilia Perez 
  - La vaginoplastiaaaa
2. Not a movie but the speech scene at the start of Bridesmaids was so cringe I felt secondhand embarrassment. They did it so well 
  - It makes me laugh every single time
    - Really quick. Speaking of Consuelo, Lilian and I took Spanish together in high school and so I’d just like to say to you and to everyone here, Gracias 
par livare la casa. And los en a sol. Tines con….and Gracias.
3.  "What are we, some kind of Suicide Squad?"
- Deadshot -
4. 50 Shades of Grey.

Basically 90% of it is cringe, but in particular -

*"Are you going to make love to me now?"*

*"I don't make love. I fuck. Hard".*

I cringed so hard I nearly vomited 
5. "They fly now?"

"They fly now!"

Tommy Wiseau would be proud...
6. Somehow, Palpatine returned…
  - they fly now

![gif](giphy|c6DIpCp1922KQ)

## 2. Clint Eastwood turns 96 today. Happy birthday to him!

**Score:** 715

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I’m 63, when Clint goes my childhood will be over.
  - Becoming a senior citizen and having your childhood hero still be around is quite the accomplishment. 
2. Didn’t know I shared a birthday with Clint. Cool beans   
![gif](giphy|S3RWlcocjbN5M5gkD1)
3. ![gif](giphy|3o6ZtoGTm2HwiKxhUk)
4. ![gif](giphy|75KBld8VOwdu8)
5. “I know what you’re thinking, did he fire 6 shots or only 5?”
6. I wonder if he’s still talking to that chair? He should have stuck to movies and not politics. 
7. He will never read reddit but HAPPY BIRTHDAY!
8. ![gif](giphy|5AWyS0JYoBrmE)
9. Did it "make his day"

## 3. Favorite “protagonist reveals their skills” moments?

**Score:** 682
**Body:** I’m a sucker for any movie moments where one of the main characters reveals their skills for the first time. For example: in Snatch when they show that Mickey is a Gypsy bare knuckle boxing champion. Any other moments come to mind?

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. https://i.redd.it/zf4b25jw3h4h1.gif
  - You seem a decent fellow. I hate to kill you.
    - You seem a decent fellow.  I hate to die.
2. It turned out that the sweet-talking, tattoo-sporting pikey was a gypsy bare-knuckle boxing champion. Which makes him harder than a coffin nail.
  - Plus the song choice in that moment 

![gif](giphy|3o7aCWJavAgtBzLWrS)
3. The Guest, the Bourne films, Colin Firth in Kingsman.
4. everything Betty Gilpin does in the Hunt

https://preview.redd.it/8y66je674h4h1.jpeg?width=1777&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=5e268207045d206876bd84784389885f8be63fa5
5. Lemme say Viggo Mortensen in A History of Violence. 

Such a “meek” guy, till he’s forced to act. Then when he does it tells you everything you need to know about him. 
6. The the first fight scene in Equalizer was surprisingly good

https://preview.redd.it/p3z5tymv4h4h1.jpeg?width=480&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=2c800ee0692156e6db99db6514abd60b5f0cb811
7. Oh in Sherlock Holmes when he breaks down the fight before showing it full speed 

https://preview.redd.it/uuovavjl8h4h1.jpeg?width=1280&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=2d276e4352a7c328f5d4ae39e47a1eefad251091

## 4. What's the most memorable final piece of dialogue you've heard in a movie? I'll start with Red's final lines in The Shawshank Redemption.

**Score:** 683

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. "Now we are free. I will see you again my friend. But not yet. Not yet."


Djimon Hounsou's delivery is perfect.


And thus ended the Gladiator franchise in my head.
2. "Son of a bitch, he stole my line..."
3. "I never had any friends later on like the ones I had when was twelve. Jesus, does anyone?"

~ STAND BY ME
4. "You met me at a very strange time in my life." 
*Where is my mind...
5. “That’ll do pig. That’ll do.” Or something like that, from Babe.
6. Fun fact the movie was supposed to end on the bus ride but they added the beach because it was too much agony not to have a reunion.
7. You see us as you want to see us—in the simplest terms, in the most convenient definitions. You see us as a brain, an athlete, a basket case, a princess, and a criminal. Correct? That's the way we saw each other at 7:00 this morning. We were brainwashed... But what we found out is that each one of us is a brain, an athlete, a basket case, a princess, and a criminal. Does that answer your question?

Sincerely yours, 

The Breakfast Club
8. The Thing

"Why don't we just wait here for a little while... see what happens..." - MacReady

![gif](giphy|3ohc13G394pSFZh5Ac)
9. Charles S. Dutton as Dillon from Alien 3:

"We're all gonna die. The only question is when. This is as good a place as any to take our first steps to heaven. The only question is how you check out. Do you want it on your feet? Or on your fucking knees..."

He's got all kinds of great lines in the film. I really adore Alien 3. It's not my favorite of the franchise, but it's endearing in an oddly twisted kind of way.
10. Some Like It Hot had the original last line zinger.

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. One pet peeve I have with some writing in some dramas is that they completely lack levity in conversations. Everything is serious, nothing is casual.

**Score:** 96
**Body:** Note: This mentions the movie sentimental value, but there is truly nothing spoilery here at all outside of discussing the tone of the movie and a few small throwaway lines. If you really want to go in COMPLETELY blind, don't read. 

This is something I really noticed while watching sentimental value (which I adored). So many scenes of complete and utter seriousness without even a hint of basic conversational levity or banter between characters. Obviously some scenes did (nora with her semi-bf for instance). 

I get that they don't want to dull down the dramatic aspects of the scripts. They want people to be taking this seriously as a drama. But the reality is that conversations rarely work that way. People make small little jokes, say sarcastic or exaggerated things, use idioms, make sassy or ironic or witty comments etc to create an atmosphere of friendliness. Things to make the other person smile, even slightly. That is just how people are. Even in northern europe, where conversational levity is definitely less than, say, new york, it is still very common. 

the specific scenes which really showed this to me were between elle fanning and stellan in sentimental value. From start to finish, they do not make even a slight comment which could be considered levity. The only two slight ones I can think of is stellan telling her to get on the horse carriage, and him saying "to what do I owe the honor" in a casual tone near the end. That is it. 

I think a lot of screenplays really can benefit from adding some more naturally 'casual' conversations in between the dramatic conversations. These are humans, they are not just big bags of emotions.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. If I Had Legs I'd Kick You, Sorry Baby, still winning

I think it was Kubrick who said comedy is more realistic than tragedy, because it allows room for the absurd. I really feel that, you need a little breadth in emotional range, and some funny moments like those above really adds to the punches later 

No Other Choice and The Mastermind hit the same way for me, and most of Paul Thomas Anderson's scripts.
  - Sorry Baby went a bit too far with it imho
2. This point is totally fair, but I do want to come to Sentimental Value’s defense here. Gustav is a deeply pretentious art film director and Nora is a neurotic actor with serious depression whose neuroses stem at least partially from her father’s behavior. Add Rachel Kemp into the equation, a celebrity actor insecure about her own place as a serious artist, you have a pretty cut and dry scenario for Serious Drama™️ to be constantly playing out. Not that Agnes is an unserious character but her relative normalness compared to all these art freaks is part of what makes the big scene near the end between the sisters so moving for me. I grew up in a family of neurotic artsy people and I can attest it do be like that sometimes.
3. I think comedy or slice of life is what breathes life into a drama. We are almost never stuck in one emotion or one mood. The very nature of emotions are that they are temporary and at the very least they fluctuate. Writing comedy also requires wit.


I think for me, most life-changing movies are categorized as Comedy/Drama for these reasons. Happy Go Lucky is one of my all-time favorites.
4. This is why I love Mad Men. Humor and a lot of variety slipped into an otherwise serious show. 

I haven’t seen the movie you refer to. It probably must be hard for a movie screenwriter, because they’re trying to cram often a book’s worth of a story into 2 hours, so they naturally cut out anything more casual. With tv they can naturally have more room to breathe
5. I think a lot of people share this peeve, whether they know it or not, and I think it's why everyone got so excited about Tarantino when he came on the scene. 

The intro to *Reservoir Dogs*, which is of course his directorial debut, is exactly what you're describing: just people talking the way people do. 

I think it was a breath of fresh air, and something a lot of viewers didn't know they wanted. 

He's often praised for his well-written dialogue, and was the first person to come to mind when I saw your comment. (He does other stuff very well too, but this is a conversation specifically about dialogue.)

Anyway my point is, I think people in general want the same thing you do. I have to stop here because I'm coming off like a Tarantino fanboy, and that's one of the most annoying things a person can be, short of being Tarantino himself. 

Hehe. I'm more of a Michael Haneke man. Although his films are usually SO dramatic that he's irrelevant to this conversation.
6. I have the same gripe about Christopher Nolan movies. 

But If I had legs I’d kick you was amazing and it had those casual and sometimes funny conversations. The Drama also had that as well. 
7. > But the reality is that conversations rarely work that way. 

Practically no movie has real life dialogue. Real life dialogue is slow, it goes off onto tangents, it would make for a terrible film. Even a documentary has to cut around real life dialogue to make sense.

That doesn't negate your point about wanting levity. Maybe they aren't good at writing jokes. Maybe, since everything is stylized anyway, they think that for two hours we can do without levity. 

The other thing to remember is that screenplays are very restricted with dialogue. Often you need to use the bare minimum. And you have to keep the flow. And you started adding jokes you're going to derail the tone, and then you have to do more work to get it back.

Movies are trying to simplify so that their meanings can be more expensive. I don't think it would make every movie more enjoyable to be putting in jokes.

Also, many of of these characters require it for the character to work.
8. Spoilers for Sentimental Value but

>!I think that's just good character work? Gustav feels less comfortable and joking around Rachel, but he doesn't have the same issue with everybody. I thought it was very intentionally funny when he admitted that he was lying to Rachel about the story and the ikea stool, and I thought the DVDs were in this same register of dark joke. He was a funny guy, just not able to be so universally because his sense of humor is fucked up. He is restraining himself in polite company.!<
9. The problem you're describing is one of dynamics. Comedy and levity contrasts with drama and seriousness, and those contrasts make the story more dynamic. Drama works by building and releasing tension, and if it's serious all the time, it's hard to build drama. You can only increase the tension so much before the audience stops feeling it. 

Likewise, comedy works better when there is tension or stakes underneath it all. Even a joke a minute movie like Airplane or Naked Gun is contrasted by the dramatic situation (a plane with no pilot/a police investigation) and the tropes of the serious genre they are parodying. The actors play it like a straight drama and that makes the comedy funnier (compare it to later Leslie Nielsen films where he was clearly doing the "hey look at me, I'm funny" and it's not as funny). 

And of course in real life, it's rare that people talk for more than a few minutes without cracking a joke. 

## 2. The longevity of James Bond.

**Score:** 30
**Body:** The first James Bond movie came out in 1962. 25 "official" Bond movies from Eon Produtions have released since then. Which means Bond has been relevant for over 60 years. It is fascinating how this character and franchise has endured and withstood the test of time across decades and multiple films. Usually series lose steam and start losing audiences.

However, that is not the case with Bond. With the exception of a couple of Roger Moore films and the Dalton films, which had middling grosses, Bond movies have always been big hits. Craig's movies continued the success and Skyfall actually gave the franchise their first billion dollar film.

I think the endurance of Bond is very interesting in two ways. 

Firstly, the series seems to be in communication with the times. Like the earlier films seem to be very much inspired by the times and culture around them. The Dalton movies for example reflect the gritty and angsty feeling of 80s cinema. The Brosnan movies seem to be embracing the hi-tech futuristic vibe which was popular at the turn of the century. The Craig movies seem to take note from Jason Bourne in the 'realistic' and grounded approach. Later on, as universes and what not had become popular in the 2010s, the films started becoming narratively coherent.

Another way they seem to reflect the times is how the character has gradually changed. From Connery's heavily misogynistic and larger than life form to Craig's much more acceptable and flawed version.

This malleability of the character I feel is one of the chief reasons for his success. The character can change as long as he is an efficient spy who travels the world, seduces women, says witty lines, drinks martinis and drives fast cars.

The second interesting point is the accumalated iconic status of the character. On paper it's a silly, cartoon-ish character (although Ian Fleming wrote him with more depth than shown in the movies). But the role has taken a lot of importance. The casting of Bond is always the most speculated casting in the industry. You always have prestigous sponsors wanting to be shown in a Bond film. The premeires of Bond films have members of the royal family of UK attend them. And Bond has also been popular in men's fashion. 

It's a very fascinating thing to me how the character has withstood the test of time and quite a few middling to poor films in between. There are other examples like Frankenstein, Godzilla, King Kong and lately superheroes etc. But Bond has always maintained constant presence unlike the others.

Is Bond's longevity impressive? Do you feel he would be a relevant character going forward, since it seems the younger audiences aren't really fussed about the series/character?

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. One advantage certain franchises (James Bond, Jurassic Park, pre-2015 Star Wars) currently enjoy over others (Marvel, DC, post-2015 Star Wars) is that they occasionally went away for a while.

If there was a new James Bond movie every year, yeah, people would get sick of him.
  - Yeah i've believed for a while that if the MCU had went away post endgame/Covid and started back up last year the situation would be so much different. But have to keep the gravy train going I guess.
    - I think the whole “universe” idea that follows all IP now is a big part of the problem. In theory it sounds great for fans. In practice, it’s too often underdeveloped characters and ideas drafting on the vibes of the original. 
  - maybe I'm wrong but until relatively recently, there was a special place for franchises like Bond and Star Wars because the vast majority of films were more original works. It seems like Hollywood is flooded with franchises and remakes these days so I for one am much more jaded towards anything resembling a franchise at this point. I also believe many of the installments of these franchises simply rely on the name alone to hit a pre-determined dollar amount and the quality is only enough to make it there, which is usually pretty subpar most of the time
2. Casino Royale is the reason Bond is still taken seriously post-2000, and the reason that the franchise is viewed as more than just a campy cold war relic. It can't be overstated how important it is that Craig's Bond starts out on what is not only pretty much a perfect action movie, but one which thoughtfully examines what Bond looks like in the modern world. Whether that inertia carries forward for a new Bond is anyone's guess.

>since it seems the younger audiences aren't really fussed about the series/character?

The new video game sold a million and a half copies day 1.
3. I love Bond, but I'm *very* skeptical about the future. The Broccolis have been the constant, steady hand guiding Bond throughout the years. Now, they've sold out to Amazon and Jeff Bezos (an actual, real-life Bond villain with rockets and everything) will likely water it down, dilute it with spinoff series, and optimize it for streaming. I hope I'm wrong, but I have zero trust in Amazon to properly steward it.

They've done a smart thing in hiring Denis Villeneuve to direct the first one out of the gate, but how much control will he actually have?
  - They could definitely be looking to start strong with a Villeneuve Bond and then wring it dry with garbage spinoffs, very disconcerting. But on the other hand giving Denis the reigns could easily make the next one the best Bond film ever.

I personally doubt that Denis would agree to make a film without (almost) full control at this point. Obviously they would agree on the artistic direction before going ahead though. We wont find out till the movie comes out I guess,  but I have high hopes.
4. I’d really like to see the Bond series go back to being a series of semi-related adventures, with a few recurring characters. It’d be refreshing to have a series that’s less focused on lore and continuity and more on variety. Ironically, Knives Out, Daniel Craig’s current series, is a great template for how to do that. There’s enough flexibility that you can have one that’s a family drama, one that’s a tech satire, and one that’s a gothic thriller without losing the formula that works.
5. The new James Bond video game “First Light” just sold 1.5 million copies in like 24 hours and seems to be received well enough by fans and critics- Bond’s malleability applies to different mediums as well, not just his general depiction. 

I think the character will still have relevancy going forward, though I’m not sure how much of that will be in theatrical films. 
6. James Bond’s longevity is one of cinema’s most impressive achievements. Actually veri few franchises remain relevant for over 60 years while continuously attracting new audiences. The key is adaptability:) James changes with the times without losing his core identity. Each actor reflects the era they inhabit, from Connery’s confident Cold War spy to Craig’s more vulnerable and grounded interpretation. The franchise also benefits from its prestige and event status, avoiding oversaturation. While younger audiences may not be as invested today, Bond has repeatedly reinvented itself whenever it seemed outdated. If future films continue evolving with cultural trends, James Bond’s relevance is likely to endure.

## 3. The Baby (1973) and Girly (1970) - the horror of not growing up

**Score:** 29
**Body:** Before I catch up on the new 2026 horrors, I decided to go through some random films on my list, and I inadvertently ended up watching two movies back to back that have some thematic similarity. This made me realize how much I enjoy the craziness of the subgenre that involves insane families, insane family rituals, and people being stuck in inane family dynamics. 


**The Baby** is a movie about a family that consists of mom, two hot sisters, and an adult son everyone refers to as The Baby. The family has decided that he was developmentally disabled and they treat him as a literal baby. He also behaves and sounds like a baby. A social worker becomes obsessed with that case, seemingly because she believes he is a potentially normal person who was just never allowed to develop. 

The movie premise alone is very creative, and it is a fun and bizarre watch. I also appreciated the silently competent protagonist, they spared us having to watch her put things together slowly, pretty much what is clear to the viewers is clear to the character and the plot progresses quickly. The movie keeps it up until the end, and I really didn’t expect it to end the way it did, even with the clues we were given (in many ways it was a simpler ending than some of my theories, yet still pretty wild, and in it, surprising.) 

There’s something insane about listening to all these women genuinely talk to this adult guy as if they were talking to a baby, and interestingly no one (even a random babysitter who has a very special moment with The Baby) shows any disgust at the Baby himself, they're really very committed. It affects your perception too, so the character of The Baby really comes across as a very innocent victim, and in all the insanity you can feel bad for him. 

 While it doesn’t overkill with the psychology, there is a scene where mom explains to the guests at a party how having a kid like that is both a blessing and a curse, which gives some insight into her Munchausen by proxy mentality, but there is still enough to keep you guessing. The two crazy, hot sisters are just a fun addition to the overall dynamic, also infantile in their own way but allowed to be adult. The movie shows enough to get the picture, but leaves enough to keep thinking about it. 

I would even say that despite being very good, I could see value in remaking a movie like this, I just think there’s so much potential to do even more with this premise. But maybe that would kill the absurd charm the movie has. As is, the story is paced well, it manages to surprise you, and the overall insanity all the characters in their way participate in is done really well. You can watch it on youtube. 

**Girly** or, “Mumsy, Nanny, Sonny and Girly” is British, and it’s such a surprisingly great movie. The aesthetics of it, the actors, and the story all create this great faintly surreal atmosphere of an insane brutal childhood game or a fairy tale. The premise is that the four title characters make for a “happy family” and the son and daughter (sonny and girly) often go out to hunt for a new friend. The new friend is then brought to the home where they’re forced to play games and follow inane rules set by Mumsy and Nanny, but enforced by everyone. 

The character of Nanny also reminds me of the Nanny from Blackadder in her behavior. Mumsy is a surprising take on the psycho mom, pleasantly calm and self-occupied. Sonny is just a brat son who doesn’t know what he’s dealing with in his own family, and Girly is really the star character, to the point where you get why despite acting insane she gets “new friends” to go home with her.

The development of the story is incredibly original because the protagonist immersed in this insanity actually takes a surprising approach to the game. So instead of being the expected normie desperately trying to get out, you get something much more fun. All the character reactions and interactions in this movie are hilarious, unexpected, and in a weird way still manage to be believable. 


The movie is also available on youtube. I think any remakes would ruin this one, it captured something special. 

**Here are some other movies that fit the theme which I also recommend:**
 
Dogtooth - my favorite Lanthimos movie, contains both the parents that didn’t let the kids grow up, or do so in particular ways, and the bizarre family rituals and logic the parents set for the kids 

Braid - friends instead of family, but it’s about 3 adult girls trapped into their childhood games in a mansion, a really good movie that I don’t see mentioned much

Devil’s Rejects/House of 1000 corpses - while the adult children aren’t necessarily forced to stay infantile, it is about a weird murderous family, and the character of Baby has that element of childishness mixed with brutality, reminiscent of Girly. Excellent, fun movies too.

 American Gothic - not as good as the others but a fun slasher, it contains cruel old fat children, and it is more grotesque in that sense than some of these other movies because they look really repulsive too. That ruins the hidden appeal factor for me though, which I will elaborate on below, and makes the family more purely antagonistic/villainous 

What is interesting in these movies is that underneath the insanity or horror of living that way, there is an element to it that I think many viewers would find secretly appealing. The Baby is too extreme for that factor (the experience of being a baby hardly seems enjoyable), but to paraphrase the mom in the movie, she asks the question, isn't it nice to stay in that protected world of childhood and not have to deal with being a grown up. In some cases it is the parents that don’t want things to change, in some cases the children can see it as a better alternative to adult life too. At the very least most people probably experienced moments where some existing family/friends/whatever dynamics started to change while they wanted to preserve them as they are. 

The surreal horror that comes from this need to preserve the status quo is especially interesting in how it combines something familiar with something that doesn’t belong. Balancing this hidden appeal with the grotesqueness of the situation is I think the key in making it work well, but sometimes the originality alone is enough to make it a good watch.


EDIT: Whatever Happened To Baby Jane? should also be on the similar movies list as probably the OG of the genre, and really one of the best movies of all time, with one of the best horror characters of all time, and the insanely great performance by Bette Davis.

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Have you seen, “Whatever Happened to Baby Jane?”  It has the twisted family dynamic and the very prolonged infantilism.  It terrified me as a kid and fascinated me as an adult. If you haven’t seen it, it will open up a whole new genre of horror for you.  
  - Yeah, I love that movie and Bette in in it, one of the best movies of all time. No idea why I left it out of the "similar movies" list, maybe because it's too good to get a casual mention. I'll edit it in though.
    - You should watch The Tin Drum too. Read the book first though, it's a modern classic that most of these newer films borrow from.

## 4. New Beauty: was pretty impressed with Charlize Theron's Apex performance. The Second Genre Life of "sex symbol" actresses in physical action

**Score:** 6
**Body:** She brought all the regular acting chops she usually brings to the table, and that physicality from Atomic Blonde and others, but mostly I just struck by the aura she created as a woman of 50, who is both glamorous and extremely gritty, set in a primordial, visually popping Nature tableau that felt CGI aided (or otherwise made hyper-real) so much to make it feel like a Science Fiction planet. I can't say that I enjoyed the movie, but it did leave a lasting impression and was such an interesting vehicle for her, showcasing extreme physicality and her portrayal of enduring beauty. It's kind of Deliverance meets Instagram, with high end performances. I'm not sure what to do with it. Seems like an allegory of an extremely toxic relationship (psycho Misery (1990), but with teeth), but more interesting is how actresses who may have been called sex symbols in their 20-30s have found a second genre life in the physical. I'm thinking even of Naomi Watts' excellent Infinite Storm (2022) - which is almost the anti-Apex - and Annette Bening's incredible Nyad (2023), both films which took "nature" as the adversary, and made the woman's physical and *mental* capacities the star, this is leaving aside, or going beyond all the roles in more commercial action which have arisen.

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. There is an interesting feminist portrayal here, an implied The Bear vs the Man question (and the viral internet debate), despite no bear being present, as Theron's character carefully has to >!politely navigate the encroaching dangers of men, from convenience store small talk, to the camp out hooliganry, until she is finally cornered in a primitive, Stone Age hut by a river, by a man invoking obscure rites and rituals, a man seemingly attempting to get close to his "inner Native" self through brutal hunting, murder and cannibalism!<...where she is just a woman who wants to enjoy some freedom, self-testing and Natural beauty. There is a implied critique of the primitivized modern man (the various Alphas...or as the title suggests "Apexes"), set against the liberated, extremely tough and physical modern woman.

## 5. What Have You Been Watching? (Week of (May 31, 2026)

**Score:** 7
**Body:**  Please don't downvote opinions. Only downvote comments that don't contribute anything. Check out the [WHYBW archives](https://www.reddit.com/r/TrueFilm/wiki/index#wiki_what_have_you_been_watching.3F).

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. **Castles for Two (1917, dir. Frank Reicher)**

A charming romantic comedy. Castles for Two is the story of a stubborn landlord bleeding his tenants dry and the wealthy American subverting him at every turn by supplying the tenants with all the money they need. The film plays around with the characters' identities in creative and fun ways, effectively allowing the American to transform the landlord's character into that of a more conscientious man. The dynamic is complex enough to bypass any questions about the ethics of lying for romantic leverage, and the tone is very light and pleasant. The archetypal lines in which the characters are drawn are more evocative of European fairy tails than traditional dramatic cinema, something that's amplified by the presence of fairies bringing the characters together. Overall, it's not a groundbreaking film, but it is very pleasant, and I think it holds up as a film today.

**The Clodhopper (1917, dir. Victor Schertzinger)**

The Clodhopper comes close to making a profound, interesting statement, but it ultimately falls short. The Clodhopper is the story of Everett Nelson, a young, ambitious man who's shunned by his agrarian father and makes his way to New York, where he pulls down a handsome salary dancing on Broadway in a show meant to emulate the typical rural American countryside. There's a cynical edge to The Clodhopper that I find interesting. Everett lands this job not because he auditioned for a bunch of roles across the city but because a director happened to see him and thought, pretty much at a glance, that he had the right stuff. Although Everett does come from a rural background, the show feels inauthentic, a self-referential bit of commentary about how the poor are portrayed in this practically brand-new medium we call cinema. Real poor people live lives in mediocrity, never getting the spotlight, but Everett can pull down ten thousand a week pretending to be one. In the climax, he uses his enormous profits to pay out the panicked clients of his father's rural bank, therefore preventing a minor economic collapse that reminds me, with my 110 years of hindsight, of Black Monday twelve years later. The people of Everett's old town survive off of what Everett can earn at their expense, but that cannot last.

Most, if not all, of this subtext is purely unintentional. I know this because Schertzinger likely didn't know that the American banking system was going to explode twelve years hence, and also because Everett is not treated by the text as a selfish, vain performer. He's a wide-eyed dreamer with ambitions so much bigger than his one-horse town. The problem here is that Everett is not likeable or interesting. We're not given a reason to root for him. All he has is some vague dream about not working on his father's farm, so when he slacks off, he just comes off like an entitled little prick. The father is no saint, but we don't get enough of his flaws to justify Everett's behavior. What we're left with is a kid with his head in the clouds navigating a story with no self-awareness. As interesting as The Clodhopper can seem, it's really only skin-deep at the end of the day.

**Friday the 13th (1980, dir. Sean S. Cunningham)**

My favorite YouTube channel is Red Letter Media, and I really love Best of the Worst. When I was watching Friday the 13th, I could see in it a lot of the bad slashers they cover on BOTW, and it was enough to make me wonder just why and how Friday the 13th became a cultural phenomenon. It isn't exactly original and daring; Halloween and The Texas Chainsaw Massacre had come out many years earlier, and both were technically so much better. The scares aren't very effective, and the gore really betrays its low budget. The writing is sophomoric, and the acting isn't much better. I expected this from an '80s slasher, but I did hope there would be a little more. But there isn't. Narratively, they really nailed down the formula for this genre. The best I can say about Friday the 13th is that I really liked the lazy summer atmosphere and what little we saw of the small town with its paranoia and Yankee eccentricities. All of that is original, and hard to replicate in a million-dollar horror franchise designed to make people scream as loud as they can.

**Bugonia (2025, dir. Yorgos Lanthimos)**

Bugonia is a remake of a much older Korean comedy movie called Save the Green Planet!, something I didn't learn about until after I finished the film. I thought that this made Bugonia make a lot more sense. The idea that the ultra-wealthy are aliens parading as humans is an old one, or at least one that feels like it came from a more innocent time when half of the internet posted memes of Barack Obama with lizard eyes and the other glazed Elon Musk. Bugonia takes the idea that Emma Stone is an alien pretty seriously, which, because of the dated nature of the theory, even among conspiracy theorists, felt jarring and a little contrived. But the exact theory matters less than what it says about the man posing it and the woman countering it. The characters in Bugonia are complex, wavering between sadistic and sympathetic between and even within scenes. Jesse Plemons portrays irrational desperation in a particularly compelling way, and I like the evolution of Stone's interactions with Plemons and his brother. She's such a good manipulator she might even convince you that she's bought into the brothers' bullshit or that she intends anything less than violent retribution once this is all over.

Because of this film's still recent release, I don't want to go into the ending, but I would have preferred it to be more ambiguous. But this doesn't really damage the film overall. It's still very good - tense, violent, well-paced, and just a little funny.

**Resident Evil: Death Island (2023, dir. Eiichiro Hasumi)**

The director of Resident Evil: Vendetta, the best Resident Evil movie, returns for another explosion of schlock and stupidity. This is probably the most condensed RE movie, featuring only two setpieces and a pretty laser-focused plot. Hasumi understands that you only watch RE movies for dumb action, and that's what he gives you. Don't look for a good script here. It's less bonkers than Vendetta, but I can't act like I'm not having a good time here.

**Lake Mungo (2008, dir. Joel Anderson)**

Chilling and moving. Lake Mungo has a very no frills storyline that seems perfectly suited for a documentary, where things can emerge into the plot without feeling like they were made up on the fly, but make no mistake: it's very effective at what it sets out to do. The exploration of grief is gripping, and ties into the horror aspects in very satisfying ways. Just as Alice Palmer's life and death raise more questions than they could ever hope to answer, the Palmer family's haunting shakes the characters' emotional and intellectual understanding of the world and their place within it. This is what all ghost stories should be, stories about literal ghosts and figurative ones, the shadows the lost leave on their home or the heart of a loved one. All of this culminates in a scene so illogical, so surreal, so frightening that it makes you want to turn all the lights on and wait for the sun to rise. No questions are really answered in the film, but the human core is coherent and compelling enough to tide you over. After all, in the face of true grief, what other choice do we have?
  - 
**X-Men: The Last Stand (2006, dir. Brett Ratner)**

Every Empire Strikes Back needs its Return of the Jedi, I guess. I wasn't terribly invested in the X-Men trilogy, even after X2 pleasantly lived up to its positive reputation, but this film was pretty rough. As a conclusion to the series, it's not very satisfying, throwing together Jean Grey's resurrection with an ideological tipping point between various mutant factions and the US government that doesn't actually resolve anything. The metaphor for mutations - race, religion, sexual orientation, what have you - falls apart when the idea of a cure is brought up. A cure is easy to understand for in-universe reasons, but what exactly does it represent in a broader meta argument? The film can't make up its mind, and ignores any finer subtext in favor of cool action scenes and overzealous special effects. The ultimate conclusion is that, no matter what, a mutant should choose to express and embrace themselves however they see fit, which is something that a) marginalized groups whom mutants represent already understand, and b) is not resolved on a systemic level. Can a mutant one day be elected president? Will anti-mutant prejudice actually be curbed on all levels of society? I don't know. I think the film wants me to think so, because the president is a mutant sympathizer, but the government still weaponized the cure, and exploited a mutant to get it. More work than a battle on Alcatraz needs to be done.

How does Jean fit into all this? She doesn't. She's a pawn characters vie and fight for, with no ideological stakes or real motivation. The concept of the Phoenix seems like it was invented by a guy in the 1960s who saw a woman in a tube top and developed a fear of female sexuality. Phoenix, lacking all inhibitions, wants to fuck guys until they disintegrate, and is so out of control that only Logan can stop her - with murder. It's crass and grossly unsatisfying, which I guess is consistent for this movie. Characters lack coherent motivations, have thin personalities, and lack strong dynamics. Characters like Scott and Professor X die for convenience's sake, and don't really feel earned or dramatically satisfying. Logan's last line is a joke about a character he barely knows, not a reassuring word shared with Rogue or Storm. Just because Bryan Singer was away doing Superman shit, that doesn't excuse lousy writing.

**The Kid Brother (1927, dir. Harold Lloyd)**

A cute, kinetic comedy. Harold Lloyd is excellent before and behind the camera, orchestrating delightful bits of slapstick and energetic chase scenes. Lloyd as Harry Hickory, the dim son of a county sheriff, is charming in a dopey way, and it never feels like the movie clowns on him too hard. He's given the opportunity to act on behalf of the community, and boy does he take it. There isn't too much to talk about in regards to the story, but that's the last thing you're thinking about as Lloyd slinks around a sinking steamship.

**Once Upon a Time in America (1984, dir. Sergio Leone)**

An incredible piece of cinema. Through three often intertwining timeframes, Leone explores the evolution of American organized crime from cliques governing ethnic boroughs in New York to doubletalking executives on the fringes of a flattening society. The story of Once Upon a Time in America is a complex one, touching on themes like the loss of innocence, the American dream, the melting pot, remorse, nostalgia, and ambition, among others. While OUATIA is hard to succinctly summarize, all of these ideas crystalize nicely and coherently, which within a daunting 220-minute runtime is a monumental achievement. It will take a full afternoon to watch OUATIA, but you'll never be bored or looking at the clock. If you won't be ensnared by the moral ambivalence and emotional ambiguity, you'll definitely be hooked by the filmmaking. Leone takes the tricks that served him well in the Dollars Trilogy - brilliant compositions, command of silence, liberal use of close-ups - and dials them up to eleven. OUATIA is at once epic and intimate, detailed and broad, able to mine equal drama out of five decades of degradation and five minutes of tense violence.

If I were to identify a flaw in OUATIA, it's in its treatment of female characters. The film is unrelenting in its depiction of gangsters and doesn't moralize. Rape occurs in the film, and it's brutal and horrible. I appreciate the intentions behind each rape scene, not to glorify the crime but to demonstrate just how far these assaulting characters have fallen, but problems arise when it comes to the victims' characterizations. The victims are given much less time to develop than their assaulters, and their reactions come off more as jilted lovers than someone who's had their body violated in a traumatic, violent way. As a result, these scenes are trivialized. Leone places more importance on Robert de Niro or James Woods' emotional turmoil than their victims'.

While this gives the film a nasty bruise, OUATIA is still 220 minutes, and Debra and Carol are still well-written characters. At that length, it would be hard not to have at least something going for you. I don't know if OUATIA redeems itself, but I also don't know if it needs to. The film is practically a world unto itself, and the fact that it makes sense and packs such an emotional punch matters more than almost anything.

**Oculus (2013, dir. Mike Flanagan)**

After the awkward Absentia, Flanagan finds his niche and snuggles up. Oculus is reminiscent of many of Flanagan's later works, namely The Haunting of Hill House, and while it isn't as good as that miniseries, it's not too far off the mark. Trauma and the supernatural are snugly tied together in this film, resulting in compelling character dynamics in the first half and nightmarish surrealism in the second. Karen Gillan and Brenton Thwaites are great, the editing is tight, and the story never loses its humanity even as things get scarier and scarier. This film set the tone for the rest of Flanagan's career, and I'm elated that it did.

**The Hit (1984, dir. Stephen Frears)**

A great tourist ad for Spain. Frears finds the most visually-striking locales in Spain, all the way from the arid interior to the forests of the Basque Country, and shoots them in the most visually-striking ways he can. Paired with a mellow soundtrack on, fittingly, a Spanish guitar, the direction is captivating enough to keep the viewer's attention. A lot of this, though, is due to a fairly flat story. John Hurt and Tim Roth abduct Terence Stamp on behalf of a mob boss, whom Stamp had testified against ten years ago, who intends to personally kill Stamp. For much of the film, the characters appear one-dimensional, with Hurt leaning into his tough guy persona, Roth a bit more convivial, and Stamp like a plucky tourist. There's little to attach yourself to until the third act, when Stamp lays out his ethos - he's been expecting this reprisal for so long that he feels a sense of relief, even though he knows he's going to die. It's an interesting idea for a character, but it doesn't recontextualize much of the preceding film in an interesting way, and he doesn't have enough time to really explore it before the film ends. Hurt, with his contradictory actions, gets no revelation of his own, and ends the film one-dimensional. The plot is very thin, and the only thing keeping the film up is its style, which can't patch over the rougher parts of the script, but can entertain.

**Before I Wake (2016, dir. Mike Flanagan)**

Another great outing by Flanagan. Before I Wake continues the narrative trends of Oculus, exploring the intersection of trauma and the supernatural, but tells its story in a dark fantasy context. It's like Ursula K. Le Guin's The Lathe of Heaven mixed with The Babadook. Before I Wake tells the story of Jessie, a mother who's lost her child, taking in a foster child who, as it turns out, can manifest his dreams into reality. She abuses this ability to interact with a facsimile of her deceased son, creating friction with her husband and dabbling with powers she doesn't understand. The concept behind Jessie as a character is pretty fucked up, but the film tells her her story with such emotional honesty that it's hard not to sympathize with her. We're not asked to look the other way, but only to try to understand how a parent can struggle with such a traumatic loss, how moving on is not a linear process and can hurt people if not done healthily. Through this, Flanagan creates some really spiffing sequences that are as visually interesting as they are scary. A lot of this is hurt, unfortunately, by a Psycho-like exposition dump that tries and largely fails to take the film down a sentimentalist route and undermines a lot of the metaphorical power of the film's supernatural content. You don't need to explain everything, Mike. Fortunately, a lot of his content after Before I Wake is a lot more balanced in this regard, and it is generally nice to move on from the pessimism of Absentia and Oculus.

**Ouija (2014, dir. Stiles White)**

Maybe I spoke too soon calling Friday the 13th a generic slasher. If you directed this script on a shoestring budget in 1984, it wouldn't have even won most BOTWs. Ouija is a nothing burger of a horror movie that isn't scary, isn't emotionally compelling, isn't funny, isn't anything. The only thing that's noteworthy about it is that Mike Flanagan worked on its prequel, which is apparently very good. That's the whole reason I even watched this piece of shit. I know nothing about Origin of Evil, but I have a feeling that getting all this vital context wasn't worth it.
2. Tuner.

Really like it when thrillers are based in unconventional settings and this one had a terrific backdrop. And they told it from the point of view of  a character with a hearing-related medical condition, which is complimented beautifully by both Leo Woodall's performance and Johnnie Burn's sound design.

The script surprises you with some of the choices made and along with the editing, it achieves a nice balance between the thriller aspect and the interplay between the characters (Dustin Hoffman kills with the limited screentime he has and Havana Rose Liu is terrific).
3. **The Mandalorian and Grogu (2026, Jon Favreau)** — Probably the most forgettable *Star Wars* movie to date. Nothing about it is truly terrible, but almost everything is just a little bit subpar. Feels more like an episode of the show that was padded to feature length than a movie. **4/10**

**The Cook, The Thief, His Wife and Her Lover (1989, Peter Greenaway)** — Felt like this was mostly carried by a strong central performance. I’m aware that the fact it’s so unpleasant to watch is intentional, but that doesn’t mean I have to like it, nor that I see any real point to its being such. **5/10**

**The Hunchback of Notre Dame (1996, Gary Trousdale/Kirk Wise)** — Nowhere near the best movie of the Disney Renaissance, but maybe the most fascinating. It’s not exactly breaking new ground to say that the main reason it stands out as such is the character of Frollo, perhaps the only villain in the Disney canon whom the movie actually attempts to turn into a plausible human individual, rather than being left as purely a mythic incarnation of evil. But what really intrigues me is just how little there is to make this gel with the rest of the characters in the movie, who are otherwise mostly the same broad archetypes that you find in the rest of Disney’s output from this era. I guess I could complain about it being tonally inconsistent, and I did have some problems with that, but, on the whole, I think I might actually enjoy it more because of this. Whether intentionally or not, it gives a sense that the evils of our world are attempting to break into the fairy tale setting that we’re used to, which I find to be, if not necessarily better, then at least more interesting than if the whole thing was dark and realistic throughout. Recommended. **7/10**

**King Kong (1933, Merian C. Cooper/Ernest B. Schoedsack) — re-watch** — Don’t have much to add to the last time I talked about this. Still quite possibly the greatest monster movie of all time.  **10/10**

**The Umbrellas of Cherbourg (1964, Jacques Demy) — re-watch** — Huh. After finding that I liked this movie a lot more on second viewing than I did on my first, I honestly thought that a third viewing would bump it up to a perfect rating, but, if anything, my opinion of it actually went down slightly. Honestly, despite my praise of it in my previous review, I think I’m still not really sold on the whole concept of doing the movie as a single extended musical number. I don’t mind constant singing in stories like *Les Miserables* or *Hamilton*, where the entire story is basically one long succession of massive, operatic emotions, but when Demy has characters breaking into song even for something as mundane as saying they like movies better than opera, it does start to feel like more of a gimmick than a meaningful artistic choice.

Nevertheless, this is still an amazing story, shot gorgeously, and despite my reservations about the basic concept, the movie-long-number itself is quite a remarkable feat of composition. So I’m still keeping this as a must-watch. **9/10**

**Movie of the week:** *King Kong*
4. **Moloch (2022, Nico van den Brink)**

Another Shudder film, but was pleasantly surprised. I like folksy type horror and this one set in the Netherlands was a good watch. **6/10**

**Backrooms (2026, Kane Parsons)**

Surprised by all of the hype this one is getting, breaking A24’s box office record for opening weekend! I really liked this one, they nailed the unsettling eerie feeling perfectly. Never heard of the term “liminal spaces” before, now it’s all I’m seeing. **7.5/10**

**Civil War (2024, Alex Garland)**

Really like Alex Garland, this one has been on my watchlist for a while now. It was a pretty good watch, but I found myself feeling irritated by the character Jessie more than anything. **6.5/10**

**The Good Son (1993, Joseph Ruben)**

Macaulay Culkin and Elijah Wood as children, playing the good and bad son. Macaulay was great at portraying Henry, the sociopath dangerous kid. Unsettling watch. **6/10**
